DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Nov-01-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT STATE BAR OF CALIFORNIA's DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT is sustained without leave to amend. (See Code Civ. Proc. sec. 430.10(e).)

Plaintiff filed this action, which he characterizes as a "simple billing dispute," to recover $658.50 and general damages for his "severe emotional distress" because the Bar allegedly overbilled him. (Opp. at 1:23, 5:16; Compl. 24.)

On September 1, 2017, the Bar placed defendant on inactive status because he failed to meet his MCLE requirements. (Compl., 7.) On that same day, the Bar also suspended plaintiff for failing to pay his annual license fees and outstanding penalties. (Id. 9.) Pursuant to State Bar Rule 2.33(B), the Bar assessed plaintiff with a reinstatement fee at the active rate because he was active before his suspension for failing to pay fees. Plaintiff alleges that he overpaid his re-enrollment fee and has not been properly reimbursed because his failure to complete his MCLE requirements put him on inactive status and therefore, he was supposed to be billed at the inactive rate per State Bar Rule 2.32(C). (Id. 13.)

Plaintiff fails to state a cause of action for unjust enrichment/restitution. The Bar apparently punished plaintiff for his derelictions simultaneously on the same day. Plaintiff cites no authority and offers no reason showing why he should benefit from committing two transgressions rather than merely one. In any event, the Bar's reasonable interpretation of its rules is entitled to deference since billing practices in these circumstances is within its expertise. (See Yamaha Corp. of America v. State Bd. of Equalization (1998) 19 Cal.4th 1, 12; Bus. & Prof. Code sec. 6001.1 [State Bar has "licensing, regulatory, and disciplinary functions"].)

Plaintiff fails to state a cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress. The Bar did not overbill plaintiff and this "simple billing dispute" does not allege outrageous conduct. (See Fletcher v. Western National Life Ins. Co. (1970) 10 Cal.App.3d 376, 394 [essential element of intentional infliction of emotional distress claim is "outrageous" conduct].)

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
